mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the Industrial Revolution including steam power, textile manufacturing, iron and steel production, canal and railway construction, and the factory system -- the core of section C.3.
The Iron Age covers the mastery of iron smelting replacing bronze, directly relevant to technology in the ancient world and the emergence of iron-based tools and weapons.
Cryptography spans from ancient ciphers through medieval Arabic advances to the Enigma machine and modern public-key systems, covering both medieval and 20th-century technological developments.
Directly examines the social and economic impact of information technology revolution, covering internet, digital communication, and comparisons with earlier technological revolutions.
The measurement of time from ancient sundials through mechanical clocks to atomic clocks spans the full history of technology, with precision timekeeping driving navigation, commerce, and industry.

Covers sociocultural consequences of the Industrial Revolution including urbanization, working conditions, environmental change, and social reform -- directly relevant to technology's relations with society and wealth.
The invention of radio represents a major 20th-century advance in communication technology, from Maxwell's theory through Marconi's wireless telegraphy to broadcasting.
The early history of electricity from Gilbert through Franklin to Faraday traces the birth of electrical technology, a key development in the emergence of Western technology.
The Stephensons pioneered railway technology with the Rocket locomotive and early railway lines, representing major advances in transportation and power technology during the Industrial Revolution.
The Antikythera Mechanism reveals sophisticated mechanical engineering in the Hellenistic world, demonstrating ancient Greek technological achievement with its complex gear system.
Automata span from Hero of Alexandria's pneumatic devices through medieval Islamic engineering to Renaissance clockwork, illustrating technological development across ancient and medieval periods.

Thomas Edison's inventions (light bulb, phonograph, electrical distribution, motion pictures) and the Menlo Park laboratory as the first industrial research facility represent key 20th-century technological developments.

The Needham Question asks why modern science and the Industrial Revolution developed in Europe rather than China despite China's earlier technological superiority, directly addressing the scope and comparative history of technology.
Capitalism's history from mercantilism through industrial capitalism connects technology to wealth creation and economic systems.
Coffee covers coffeehouse culture's relationship to Enlightenment discourse, journalism, and financial markets, connecting to technology and social institutions.
